food_name: Ayam Goreng Berempah (Malay Spiced Fried Chicken)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Ayam Goreng Berempah. Closest parent is deep-fried marinated chicken (FOODON:03302757 — fried chicken food product). No exact URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Deep-fried spiced marinated chicken, Malay berempah style
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0CHF is the closest FoodEx2 parent for deep-fried poultry. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Malay berempah-style fried chicken.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Ayam Goreng Berempah as a specific food entity.

allergens: gluten (possible trace from rice flour or cornstarch depending on brand)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Ayam Goreng Berempah is one bone-in, skin-on fried chicken piece weighing approximately 200g, as commonly served at Malay hawker stalls and as an accompaniment to Nasi Lemak.

nutrient_source.title: Ayam Goreng Berempah — HPB Singapore Foodo; cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD (Malaysian Food Composition Database)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; Institute for Medical Research (IMR), Malaysia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Ayam Goreng Berempah, cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD for validation. Both are approved primary regional sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Fried Chicken and Spiced Poultry Dishes — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate protein-dominant fried poultry; reference anchored to University of Sydney GI Database entries for fried chicken and mixed spiced meat dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Ayam Goreng Berempah in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) the dish is predominantly protein and fat with only 2.8g carbohydrate per 100g; (2) the closest analogous entries in the University of Sydney GI Database for fried chicken and spiced meat dishes yield GI values in the 30–40 range; (3) the presence of dietary fibre from spices further moderates glycaemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 5.6g for 200g serving) / 100 = 1.96. Confidence: 0.7 (estimated).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 420mg per 100g; a 200g serving delivers approximately 840mg sodium, representing approximately 37% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Ayam Goreng Berempah to occasional consumption (no more than once or twice per week) and pair it with low-sodium accompaniments such as plain steamed rice rather than Nasi Lemak, which adds further sodium from sambal and anchovies.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (2.8g per 100g) results in negligible glycaemic impact; GI estimated at 35 (low); glycaemic load per 200g serving is approximately 1.96.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ayam Goreng Berempah is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions due to its very low carbohydrate content and low glycaemic index, but frequent consumption (more than three times per week) should be avoided as the high fat content from deep-frying (18.8g fat per 100g) may contribute to insulin resistance over time.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.2g per 100g and cholesterol 95mg per 100g; a 200g serving delivers 12.4g saturated fat (approximately 62% of the American Heart Association recommended daily limit of 20g) and 190mg dietary cholesterol.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Ayam Goreng Berempah to occasional consumption and consider removing the skin before eating to reduce saturated fat and cholesterol intake; the anti-inflammatory spice marinade (turmeric, galangal, lemongrass) provides some cardioprotective phytochemicals but does not offset the saturated fat burden of regular deep-fried consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High total fat from deep-frying may promote systemic inflammation with frequent consumption, partially offset by anti-inflammatory curcumin and other phytochemicals in the rempah spice paste.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may consume Ayam Goreng Berempah occasionally; the turmeric and galangal in the marinade provide curcumin and other anti-inflammatory compounds, but the high saturated fat from deep-frying should be moderated to avoid exacerbating systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ayam Goreng Berempah is a beloved Malay deep-fried chicken dish distinguished from Western-style fried chicken by its intensely aromatic rempah (spice paste) marinade. The chicken — typically bone-in, skin-on pieces — is marinated for several hours or overnight in a thick paste made from fresh lemongrass, ginger, galangal, turmeric, shallots, garlic, cumin, and coriander seeds. Small amounts of rice flour or cornstarch are added to the marinade to create the signature crispy 'hampas' (spice crust bits) that form during deep-frying. Fresh curry leaves are tossed into the hot oil alongside the chicken to further infuse it with fragrance. The result is juicy, succulent chicken encased in a golden-yellow, savory, textured, and deeply aromatic coating. The turmeric imparts a characteristic golden colour and contributes curcumin even after frying. Nutritionally, Ayam Goreng Berempah provides 262 kcal per 100g, with high protein (20.5g), significant fat (18.8g, of which 6.2g is saturated) from deep-frying, and very low carbohydrate (2.8g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. Sodium is moderate at 420mg per 100g. The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is a staple accompaniment to Nasi Lemak and Nasi Kukus, and a popular standalone main dish at Malay hawker centres across Singapore and Malaysia. It is also a festive dish served during Hari Raya. Clinically, the low glycaemic index (estimated 35) and minimal carbohydrate content make it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions, though the saturated fat content warrants moderation for those with cardiovascular disease or hypertension.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo, an approved primary regional source for Singaporean dishes, cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Both are approved primary sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy. The dish is a well-documented Singaporean and Malaysian Malay hawker staple with consistent nutrient profiles across both databases. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence 0.7) as no direct GI entry exists for this specific dish.
notes: GI value of 35 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entries for fried chicken and spiced meat dishes; no direct GI measurement exists for Ayam Goreng Berempah. Glycaemic load per 200g serving calculated as 1.96 (low). Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values not reported in source; set to -1. Water content not reported in source; set to -1. Two portions are listed: 200g (1 bone-in piece, verified) and 150g (1 serving, enriched estimate). The dish is classified as gluten-free based on traditional preparation using rice flour; commercial variants may use wheat flour. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values sourced from enriched_json with confidence 0.9. Hindi local name 'मलय मसाला तला हुआ चिकन' is a descriptive translation as no established Hindi culinary term exists for this dish in Singapore/Malaysia context.
